title: Stop perfecting your prompt. Do this instead
summary: Jeff Su argues that practitioners should shift focus from prompt perfection to context engineering. He contrasts grounding and RAG as techniques that anchor AI outputs to real data, and emphasizes assembling comprehensive context over optimizing wording.
writeup: A short explainer defining three foundational AI terms: grounding, RAG (retrieval augmented generation), and context engineering. Each concept is illustrated with a concrete analogy that makes the practical implication immediately clear. The through-line is that AI output quality is less about how questions are phrased and more about what reference material and context the model has access to.
key_points: Grounding means constraining an AI to answer from a supplied document rather than from memory, which converts a probabilistic guess into a claim traceable to a source.

RAG (retrieval augmented generation) implements grounding at scale: the model first retrieves relevant material from a store, then generates its answer from that retrieved content rather than from parametric memory alone.
A practical heuristic for detecting RAG in production AI tools is whether the tool cites its sources; citation implies retrieval, the absence of citation implies the model is writing from memory.

Context engineering differs from prompt engineering in emphasis: prompt engineering focuses on the wording and structure of the question, while context engineering focuses on assembling the right background, examples, and constraints before the question is posed.
The actionable implication of context engineering is to invest effort in gathering and assembling the right reference material rather than in perfecting the phrasing of the prompt itself.
